name: Binary Heap (Priority Queue)
def: A complete binary tree, stored in an array, maintaining the heap order so the min/max is at the root.
problem: Repeatedly extract the highest-priority element and insert new ones efficiently.
mechanism: Array-embedded complete tree; sift-up on insert and sift-down on extract restore the parent ≤/≥ child invariant in O(log n).
failures: Stale entries when decrease-key unsupported (use lazy deletion)
whenNot: Need sorted iteration or fast membership
myths: "A heap is sorted" — only the root is ordered; the rest is partially ordered

x.invariant: Every parent is ≤ (min-heap) or ≥ (max-heap) its children; the tree is complete, so it packs into a contiguous array with no gaps.
x.memoryOverhead: Essentially none beyond the array — children of i live at 2i+1, 2i+2; no per-node pointers.
x.locality: Good for the array, but sift paths jump by powers of two, so deep heaps still incur cache misses.
x.mutationModel: In-place mutable; decrease-key requires an external index→position map.
x.concurrency: Not thread-safe; concurrent priority queues use skip lists or fine-grained locking instead.
